data.title: Zoom Communications Is Maintained at Outperform by Baird
Moderna Is Maintained at Underperform by Leerink Partners
Canadians Tap the Brakes After Hitting the Stores in June — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Indications of a pullback in retail spending by Canadians last month following a rebound in June continues a stretch of choppy trade and could signal that household consumption is cooling amid the stresses of the trade conflict with the U.S.

An advance tally of receipts indicates retail sales fell 0.8% from the month before in July, Statistics Canada said Friday.

The data agency offered no details with the forecast, which was based on responses from roughly 55% of retailers surveyed and will be revised.

The pullback, after a solid 1.5% increase in retail trade in June that took back the drop in May, marks a weak start to the third quarter as economists look for signs what has so far been resilient consumer consumption is cracking under the weight of trade uncertainty and the Trump administration's tariffs.

"While June's widespread gains are encouraging, it seems that Canadians didn't continue this shopping spree in July," said Florence Jean-Jacobs, principal economist at Desjardins. "Indeed, sales volumes likely contracted in July, since seasonally adjusted goods prices were flat month-to-month."

Jean-Jacobs estimates the three‑month annualized rate of real retail sales growth is now tracking a decline of 3%, which she said is a difficult environment for retailers to pass along any tariff-related cost increases.

Canada's economy has cooled sharply since beginning the year strongly, and the central bank has forecast it contracted in the second quarter. The unemployment rate at the same time has gradually risen this year, though job losses have largely been in industries tied directly to trade such as manufacturing. Consumer and business sentiment had been edging higher from record lows in the spring, but more recently look to have weakened again after Canada was left as the only Group of Seven country that failed to clinch a deal regarding tariffs with the U.S. before President Trump's Aug. 1 deadline.

Alexandra Brown, North America economist at research firm Capital Economics, said the flash estimate for July sales is a little worrying. "That implies retail sales volumes fell by about 1%, which mean consumption growth is likely to remain muted in the third quarter," she said.

The softness strengthens arguments the Bank of Canada will resume lowering interest rates this year to support the economy, though there remain plenty of economic indicators due for release before the governing council's next meeting Sept. 17. The possibility the central bank will prioritize economic and labor market weakness increased after data earlier this week showed annual inflation cooled in July and has been below the bank's 2% target four months in a row.

The growth for retailers in June meant sales rose a modest 0.4% in the second quarter of the year, and were slightly stronger in volume terms after an increase of 1.5% in June. That compares with an increase of 1.1% in nominal terms in the first three months.

June's jump brought retail volumes back to where they had stood in April, and up just 0.2% since the end of 2024, said Michael Davenport, senior Canada economist at Oxford Economics. "Momentum in consumer spending will wane in the second as job losses and higher prices from tariffs squeeze disposable income, particularly once most temporary counter-tariff relief expires in mid-October and the tariff pass-through to retail prices accelerates," he said.

Some sales data suggest a more positive start to the third quarter. Cardholder tracking by Royal Bank of Canada found customer spending rose 1.1% during July to reverse a contraction the month before, with a solid recovery in spending on travel as outlays on discretionary services and goods rose meaningfully from average levels in the second quarter. The bank, Canada's largest, expects moderate growth in consumer spending for the remainder of the year.

Statistics Canada said feedback from retailers found that 27% of businesses were affected by trade tensions between Canada and the U.S. in June versus 32% the month before. The most common impacts cited continued to be price increases, change in demand for products, and delays in the supply chain.

The survey for June showed sales were up in each of the nine industry segments the agency tracks, led by food and beverage sales and increased spending on clothing, jewelry, luggage and leather goods.

Sales at motor vehicle and parts dealers edged up 0.2% for the month, following a 3.4% drop in May, while gasoline sales increased for the first month in the last four.

Core retail sales, which exclude car and auto-parts dealers and gas stations, were up 1.9% between May and June. Stripping out just the auto sector, sales for the latest month were also 1.9% higher, stronger than the 0.9% rise economists expected.

Monthly retail sales data have been somewhat more volatile than usual this year as tariffs and the U.S. trade dispute may have shifted buying patterns, though overall goods spending has trended higher on average in 2025, said Veronica Clark, an economist at Citi. "Volatility due to trade disruptions could continue in the coming months, but ultimately we continue to expect a weakening labor market and slowing population growth will put downward pressure on aggregate spending in the second half."

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: US small caps, housing stocks rally as Powell opens door to rate cuts
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall St. indexes rally nearly 2%
  • Small-cap index touches 2025 highs
  • Airlines, retail stocks among top gainers

Rate-sensitive stocks rallied on Friday after U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell hinted that an interest-rate cut could be on the table in September, citing a shifting balance of risks.

Traders now see a 90% chance of a rate cut next month compared with about 75% before Powell's remarks.

After cutting rates by 50 basis points in September 2024 and 25 points in November and December, the central bank has held steady.

However, growing bets of a cut next month helped homebuilders outperform the broader market recently and powered gains in the shares of banks and retailers.

Here is a closer look at how some of the rate-sensitive stocks have fared since the Fed kicked off its rate-cutting cycle last year.

HOMEBUILDERS

The housing market is significantly dependent on mortgage rates, which remain elevated and have strained demand for new homes. Recent data showed even though groundbreaking for new single-family homes picked up in July, total permit issuance - a guide for future activity - fell to a five-year low.

An index tracking homebuilders jumped nearly 4% on Friday. Its rally had cooled late last year after the Fed lowered its forecasts for the number of cuts it could deliver this year and acknowledged that a lot of Trump's policies could prove to be inflationary.

But rising rate-cut expectations have renewed interest in housing stocks in recent months, and the index is on track for its biggest one-month jump since July 2024. However, analysts have warned that multiple interest-rate cuts are needed to fully revive the sector.

BANKS

The picture is more complicated for banks.

Lenders usually make more money when interest rates rise because they can charge borrowers more for loans. But if competition for deposits heats up, banks may need to raise the interest they pay to savers, which pushes up their funding costs and eats into profits.

Lenders also feel pressure when the U.S. Treasury yield curve flattens or inverts. Since banks borrow at short-term rates and lend at long-term rates, a smaller gap between the two reduces their profit per loan. A steep yield curve has the opposite effect, widening margins.

The yield curve has been steepening - meaning the gap between short-term and long-term interest rates is widening - as short-end bond yields fall on growing expectations that the Fed could resume its cutting cycle.

The S&P 500 banks index (.SPXBK) added 2%, while KBW regional banking index advanced 4.1%.

SMALL-CAPS

Small-cap companies are largely reliant on external borrowing to fund their operations, and lower borrowing costs increase their available capital.

Lower rates could also enable smaller companies to refinance their existing debt more cheaply, enabling them to then direct a chunk of their earnings to fuel growth and expansion.

The small-cap Russell 2000 index jumped 3.8% to its highest level of this year. After hitting a record high in November, the index has since underperformed Wall Street's S&P 500 as the Fed had taken a cautious stance on interest rates.

UTILITIES

Shares of utility providers are often traded as bond proxies, given their steady stream of earnings regardless of the economic situation. The sector has enjoyed gains of late as government bond yields fell on growing expectations of Fed rate cuts.

The yield on U.S. 2-year Treasury note (US2YT=RR), which reflects investors' near-term rate expectations, extended its fall after Powell's remarks.

Since the last rate cut in December, the utilities sub-index has advanced more than 15%, hitting a record high this month. Power companies Constellation Energy and Vistra have led gains on hopes they could see a surge in demand from energy-intensive data centers needed to develop AI technology.

CONSUMER STOCKS

Lower borrowing costs typically boost consumer spending, which makes up about 70% of the U.S. economy. That's good news for retailers.

During the first quarter, fears that tariffs would fan inflation and hurt consumer spending resulted in the biggest quarterly decline for the S&P 500 consumer discretionary index since March 2022.

Since the end of March this year, however, the index has jumped almost 16% through May as economic data point to resilient retail sales.

Shares of retailers such as Nike , Home Depot and Best Buy climbed between 3.1% and 4%. The S&P consumer discretionary index rose 1.1%.

Prospects of higher spending also lifted shares of airlines and credit card firms. The S&P 1500 airlines index (.SPCOMAIR) added 3.6%, while American Express jumped 4%.

GROWTH STOCKS

Interest-rate cuts boost growth and technology stocks, whose valuations rely on future earnings as lower rates increase the present value of those expected profits.

All the Magnificent Seven stocks - Apple , Nvidia , Amazon.com , Microsoft , Meta Platforms , Tesla and Alphabet - were higher, led by Tesla's 5.1% climb.

data.title: Brazilian Real Approaches Near 1-Year High
data.description:
The Brazilian real strengthened past 5.42 per US dollar, moving back toward the near one-year high of 5.39 seen on August 12th as the dollar slid sharply after Fed Chair Jerome Powell signalled the possibility of a September rate cut at Jackson Hole.

That DXY weakness, paired with a still-wide Brazil US yield spread because the Selic remains at 15%, made short-term carry into reais more attractive and drew capital back into local markets.

However, political and fiscal risks continue to cloud the outlook.

A Supreme Court ruling that foreign laws do not apply domestically was widely read as hardening the Brazil US impasse tied to Magnitsky related sanctions and Washington’s 50% tariff package on Brazilian goods, introducing legal uncertainty that keeps risk premia elevated.

data.title: Corey Feldman Honors The Beatles With New Single "Retro Rock" and Upcoming EP Inspired by Every Era of the Fab Four
data.description:
Inspired by Every Era of the Fab Four

LOS ANGELES, CA / ACCESS Newswire / August 22, 2025 / Actor, musician, and pop culture icon Corey Feldman is celebrating a new creative chapter with the release of his brand-new single, "Retro Rock," the 2nd single and opening track of his upcoming Beatles-inspired EP. The collection marks Feldman's first major release since Love Left 2.1 four years ago, and it's already making waves-the project's 1st hit single, "Characters," is currently climbing Mediabase's Top 100 charts, and this firecracker of a rock song is expected to light up its heels.

With "Retro Rock" kicking off the project, which is releasing only 10 days later, Feldman pays tribute to The Beatles by artistically brushing upon the distinct phases of their legendary career. The EP explores sounds and styles inspired by each era, from the early days through the psychedelic years and beyond. Fans can expect anything from nods to Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band, to a sweeping Abbey Road-inspired medley (three songs cut into one), which closes out this conceptual piece of work. Feldman's next single-reflecting the Beatles' 1965 era-will be released September 10, followed by the full EP's release on September 22.

Speaking on his newest EP, Feldman says: "This project is my love letter to The Beatles. Their music shaped who I am as an artist, and I wanted to honor every era of their journey while creating something fresh for my fans. And with Adrien's debut right around the corner, there's so much more to come from Ci-Fi in the months ahead."

But that's not all. As Feldman expands his creative empire with his independent label Ci-Fi Records, he is also preparing to introduce fans to his first signed artist, Adrien Skye. Her debut single, which will be co-written and produced by Feldman and his writing/production partner Gregg Sartiano, will drop on Feldman's Ci-Fi label this October. This jaw-dropping vocal powerhouse is sure to catch her own deserved viral fire, officially marking the launch of Ci-Fi Records' debut breakout artist-and leading to the possibility of more artist projects down the road.

And if all of that wasn't enough, there's a 3rd EP project on the release slate for Ci-Fi Records this year. However, that is all we are authorized to announce at this time. Stay tuned for more updates, and please check out www.coreyfeldman.net for the latest news.

MEDIA CONTACT:

Samantha Waranch

E-mail: samantha@c-360.agency

SOURCE: Truth 22 Productions Inc

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Visa shutters US open-banking unit as data fight heats up, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
Payments processor Visa has shut its open-banking business in the United States, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The business gives fintechs easier access to customer bank data, helping them offer smoother sign-ups and money transfers.

Disputes between banks and fintechs over access to customer data have fueled uncertainty in the sector.

A Bloomberg report in July said JPMorgan Chase had informed fintechs they would have to pay potentially hefty fees to access its customer data. PNC Financial CEO Bill Demchak has also said his bank was considering such a move.

Banks say such fees are necessary to recoup the cost of safeguarding and delivering customer data, while fintechs argue that banks should not be allowed to charge for data that belongs to customers, and the fees would slam their business.

On Thursday, the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau kicked off a rewrite of its regulations governing consumer control over the sharing of their personal data between banks and fintechs.

Visa did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment on the Bloomberg report.

data.title: Belarus leader dampens hopes for mass prisoner release after Trump's appeal
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump urged release of 1,300 prisoners
  • Lukashenko calls them 'bandits' who could 'wage war'
  • Opposition says Trump put veteran leader in tight spot

Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko on Friday dampened hopes for a mass release of prisoners that U.S. President Donald Trump requested from him in a phone call last week.

The Belarus opposition and human rights groups say up to 1,300 political prisoners - a figure also cited by Trump - are being held in Belarusian jails. Many of them were arrested when he cracked down on mass protests after a disputed election in 2020.

Lukashenko, in power since 1994, denies there are political prisoners in Belarus and described the people in question as "bandits".

"We will release them, and they will again wage war against us? Society won't support me on this," he was quoted as saying by state news agency Belta.

Lukashenko is a close ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin and has faced years of isolation and sanctions from the West because of his human rights record and backing for Moscow's war in Ukraine.

But Trump's surprise call last week, followed by social media posts in which he called Lukashenko "the highly respected President of Belarus" and said he hoped for the early release of the 1,300 prisoners, have opened up the possibility of a thaw in relations.

Franak Viacorka, chief advisor to exiled Belarusian opposition leader Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya, said Trump's intervention was a "big present" to Lukashenko but it had also sharply raised the stakes and increased pressure on him.

"Lukashenko is in a tough position... If he denies (the request), he will go into conflict with Trump. If he agrees, then he loses his last and only bargaining chip - political hostages," Viacorka told Reuters.

He said Lukashenko saw the high-profile detainees - including Nobel Peace Prize winner Ales Bialiatski and prominent opposition leader Maria Kalesnikava - as a threat to his hold on power. "He's afraid if these people are free and stay (in Belarus), there will be another wave of protest."

Lukashenko on Friday rejected the idea of talking to the opposition, saying he had nothing to discuss with them. He also said that he had told the Americans he would break off dialogue with them if they engaged with his political opponents.

"I warned the Americans right away: as soon as we learn that you are starting to play along with them, our fugitives, and are starting to play some kind of game here together, we will immediately stop all negotiations with you," he said.

Viacorka, however, said that Tsikhanouskaya's camp had been in contact with both the State Department and White House officials since Trump's call with Lukashenko.

"I don't think, no matter what Lukashenko wants, that the Americans will stop talking to us," he said.

JANUS HENDERSON FUND MANAGEMENT UK LIMITED

22 August 2025

THE CITY OF LONDON INVESTMENT TRUST PLC

 

Sale of Shares from Treasury

The Company announces the sale of 475,000 ordinary shares of 25p each, from shares held in Treasury. These shares were sold today for cash at a price of 510.0p per share.

Following the above sale, the Company's issued ordinary share capital will continue to be 502,664,868 ordinary shares of 25p each, of which 7,197,762 are held in Treasury and have no voting rights. The number of ordinary shares to be used in the calculation of voting rights is 495,467,106.

The number of voting rights in the Company is now:

a)         33,031,140 in respect of the ordinary shares (one vote for every 15 shares held);

b)         30,198 in respect of the First Preference Stock (one vote for every £10 of stock held); and

c)         29,484 in respect of the Preferred Ordinary Stock (one vote for every £20 of stock held).

Therefore, the total number of voting rights in the Company is 33,090,822.

The above figure (33,090,822) may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, The City of London Investment Trust plc under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club's Sales Slow as Shoppers' Frustrations Grow — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart

BJ's Wholesale Club Chief Executive Bob Eddy said shoppers across all income levels are more stressed, and that the recent quarter was hurt by economic uncertainty and poor weather.

Shares declined 8.%, to $97.44 in midday trading Friday, putting them on pace for their largest percentage decline in more than three years. Despite the drop, the stock has risen 18% in the past year.

Currently, consumers are frustrated with higher prices and continuing uncertainty regarding tariffs, Eddy said on a call with analysts. As a result, shoppers are more likely to trade down to private-label products, forgo discretionary purchases, respond to deals and use coupons.

"All of these are indications of a consumer that's a little bit more choosy with their dollars," Eddy said. "I think we'll see that for the remainder of the year."

Still, the warehouse club grew its revenue and same-store sales during its latest quarter. Profit rose, prompting the company to raise its fiscal-year earnings outlook.

"This is a clear validation of our value proposition at a time when families are under a seemingly endless cost pressure in what is an exceptionally dynamic environment," Eddy said.

Comparable-club sales, or those from stores and digital channels in operation for at least 12 months, climbed by 2.3% excluding fuel sales. That was below the 3.2% increase that analysts polled by FactSet expected.

The uptick in comparable sales was driven by traffic gains, an indication that the company continues to gain share from its competitors, D.A. Davidson analysts said. However, J.P. Morgan analysts pointed to a sequential deceleration as evidence that its share gains are slowing.

Unseasonably wet and cold weather early in the quarter, particularly in the company's core Northeast and Mid-Atlantic markets, hurt sales of patio sets, grills and backyard games, Eddy said. Weather improved and sales strengthened as the quarter progressed, "but to some degree, that stuff doesn't sell if you don't sell it early in the season," he added.

For its quarter ended Aug. 2, BJ's posted a profit of $150.7 million, or $1.14 a share, up from $145 million, or $1.08 a share, a year earlier. Adjusted earnings also came in at $1.14 a share, topping the $1.09 a share that analysts projected, according to FactSet.

Revenue climbed 3.4% to $5.38 billion, but missed the $5.49 billion that Wall Street modeled. BJ's attributed the increase in part to membership gains, as the company recently surpassed 8 million total members. It also continued to benefit from higher annual membership fees, which went into effect early this year.

For its fiscal year ending Jan. 31, BJ's continues to expect comparable-club sales to rise 2% to 3.5%, compared with the 3% increase that Wall Street forecast.

The company now expects adjusted per-share earnings of $4.20 to $4.35, compared with a prior outlook of $4.10 to $4.30. Analysts are looking for $4.30.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Gold Futures Rise as Fed Chair Opens Pathway to Interest Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description: Gold futures rise as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell effectively opens the door to a September interest rate cut. Futures are up 1.2% at $3,421.40 a troy ounce, and are now on track to end the week up 1.2% after trading broadly sideways in recent weeks. Powell's speech at the Jackson Hole symposium in Wyoming noted that prospects of a sharper slowdown in the job market could reduce concerns that tariff-driven cost increases will fuel inflation, changing the outlook in a direction that could justify resuming interest rate cuts. Markets are now pricing in a quarter-percentage-point interest-rate cut in September, increasing the appeal of non-interest-bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: CLEVE FED'S HAMMACK Q&A/CNBC: WON'T JUST LOOK AT PAYROLLSC: REMAIN OPEN MINDED WHILE LOOKING AT A BROAD RANGE OF EMPLOYMENT STATISTICS
How One Wealth Manager Built a Niche Advising Retirement-Plan Participants — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Steve Garmhausen

It was a lot of work building an advisory firm that can directly manage the assets of retirement-plan participants, but Mike Bisaro says it was worth it. "Many advisors are simply waiting for people to retire and to do a rollover," says the president and CEO of $1.2 billion-asset StraightLine Group. "We feel we are in a position to help people at all different stages of their financial journey."

Speaking with Barron's Advisor, Bisaro, who leads a team of 17, explains how the Troy, Mich.-based team gets paid for managing assets in 403(b) plans and elsewhere. He explains why retirement-plan participants aren't likely to have access to alternative investments, including cryptocurrency anytime soon, despite the Trump administration's friendliness to the idea. And he talks about the perils and rewards of owning an advisory firm with his spouse.

How did you break into the industry? It all flowed through golf, believe it or not. When I was getting ready to graduate from college, I had a number of opportunities in the more traditional finance space. But I was contacted by a family acquaintance we had played golf with every once in a while. He was an advisor, and he reached out through my parents to say that he was moving into management and was interested in somebody working with his book of clients.

I had never thought about being an advisor. When I met with him, my first question was, I'm 22 years old and coming out of college basically broke. Who's going to listen to me about money? He told me that coming out of school having done the programs I'd done, I knew more about finance than I thought. And he said that this business is all about relationships. He had played golf with me since I was 12 or 13 years old, and I was always very comfortable playing with people who were 20 or 30 years older than me. I was very comfortable with those relationships, and he said that's what this business is really about.

The more I thought about it, the more I realized how good of a fit it might be. I knew I was not wired to sit behind a spreadsheet doing financial projections for 15 years before I could do something bigger in the finance world. And I liked the idea of helping people. So I started right out of school.

You own the firm with your wife, Elizabeth, who is its controller. How do you balance the personal and professional relationships? I had a feeling you might ask that. It's something we talked about deeply before we did this. We were very open with each other and agreed that we couldn't let the business challenge our relationship. It's hard sometimes because we don't separate it all the time; we do talk about business at home. But while there are challenges, I think there's also enormous opportunity. This is not my business. This is our business. And having her involved has been beneficial in so many ways. We can talk about big decisions because she understands what's going on. And she loves the details and the behind-the-scenes things and those spreadsheets and reconciling everything. I am much more comfortable on the relationship side and dealing with other kinds of decisions that she doesn't love making. Our skill sets mesh together very well and don't conflict with each other. There are always challenges, but I think us working together creates many more positives.

StraightLine occupies an interesting niche in the industry. Can you describe what your business does? We work with individuals and families, but what makes us different is that StraightLine was built to work specifically inside of the employer sponsored space — 401(k), 403(b), 457 accounts. The company was formed shortly after the Department of Labor's 2001 opinion that essentially said independent advisors should be able to manage accounts inside of employer-sponsored plans, which for many people is their largest or only investment account.

I came here shortly after the company was founded. But it was formed to work with accounts in that space — not just to advise on them but to provide direct discretionary management for individuals within their plan. There is an enormous amount of logistics to that. You have to be structured in a certain way. You need the desire to do it in a certain way. You can't be connected to the revenue of the plan itself. To be a fiduciary inside the plan you have to be structured to do certain things and to not do certain things. And we were built from the beginning to do that.

We work with any assets people have outside of plans as well. But what makes us unique is that we can extend our direct management or advice to include assets inside a plan. That allows us to establish relationships much earlier with people. Many advisors are simply waiting for people to retire and to do a rollover. We feel we are in a position to help people at all different stages of their financial journey.

Is the advisory fee that you charge to manage plan assets the same as the fee outside the plan? Yes. Our legacy structures were not always built that way. In some cases that was more of a stand-alone service, and the fee structures reflected that. The fee structures inside of plans were capped in many cases. So when we bought the firm, in 2020, we knew we needed to evolve that. It was challenging, but as a fiduciary, our fee can't be dependent on the location of the assets. Our fee starts at 0.9%, and it tiers down at certain levels.

You have a solid niche in the higher-education world. How did that come about? That started with a successful request for proposals back in 2007, from a university that wanted help with employee advisory services in their 403(b)s. We had never worked in higher ed in a significant way, so we got a crash course in how things are different in those plans. Soon I met Rob Rickey, who was essentially the liaison between TIAA and the outside investment advisory world. We hit it off and connected quickly. A lot of the tools advisors use to this day were created by his team.

Rob and I spoke regularly for years. And then in 2022 his relationship with TIAA ended. We hired him immediately as a consultant. Rob had opportunities to go to a lot of places, but after about eight or nine months, Rob came to Elizabeth and I and said he thought we could do something special if he joined us full-time. That was a huge deal for us. There are few if any people in the country who know more about working inside of retirement plans, especially in higher ed, than Rob. The fact that he had so many opportunities and chose to come to us was a big validation of what we were and are still working toward. He has helped us move forward in ways that we simply would not have been able to on our own.

What's the mechanism for getting paid for advising within a retirement account? It depends on the plan provider. Some support it seamlessly. But the plan sponsor also has to allow the functionality to exist on their plan. Many employers do, particularly in the 403(b) world.

There are a ton of stories about K-through-12 teachers getting taken advantage of in their retirement plans due to lax regulations. Do you work with this group? And what position does that put you in as a fiduciary advisor? Unfortunately the K-through-12 world, and even community colleges, is kind of the Wild West. I have seen some horrible stories and ridiculous plan structures and enormously high fees and restrictive situations. I've seen situations where there are 30 or 40 plan providers in one school district and no economies of scale and ridiculous fees and restrictions all over the place and a lot of predatory behavior — there's just no other way to say it. I could tell you stories for days. This is a huge concern of ours and I think should be a larger concern of the industry. But it simply has not received the attention it deserves. It's a very difficult area to get into and help just because of what's built into those plans in many cases.

StraightLine does not sell plans, correct? That's correct, we do not sell plans. Although a lot of times the employers bring us in to do education and the financial wellness work. And that allows us to work with, for instance, plan consultants, which often do not want to do any of the participant work. We can come in and partner with them because they know they're not in danger of losing their plan.

You've voiced skepticism that we'll see a lot of crypto and alternative investments on plan menus, even though the regulatory environment is easing around those assets. Why do you feel that way? Our role working with retirement plans means that we are often at the table during investment committee meetings. Those committees are correctly worried about a lot of things. It's their job as fiduciaries, and often with personal liability, to be exceptionally careful about all the investment options that are available to their participants. Their decisions are guided by legal precedent, and there are generally no prizes for outside-the-box thinking. That doesn't mean that they're not innovative; it just means they are exceptionally careful.

There are trillions of dollars of assets in employer-sponsored retirement plans, and of course private equity wants access. But I think that we're going to find it slow going. A pathway was cleared for guaranteed income inside of plans four or five years ago, and we're just now starting to see some of those things. So I think the adoption for private equity and crypto is going to be slow. You're probably going to see it as part of things like target-date funds before you see it as a stand-alone investment option. I think there's going to be some exposure, but it may not keep pace with what some people seem to be expecting.

You're a successful niche firm, which makes you interesting to investors and buyers. Have you thought about your options? Once you're a firm of a certain size you do get on the radar, and certainly we are known enough in the industry that there are quite a few firms that have reached out. It's a rare day that there's not multiple inquiries. Sometimes you can tell they're just working off a list, like when I get an email that says "Hello Michael F." Yes, we've considered our options. But we have a lot of things we still want to do, and we believe we have a unique path forward.

We have not entertained any of those conversations about partnerships or outright purchases. We think we've got a long way to go before we would even consider any of that. And we are very protective of what we've built. A number of employees, including myself, have been here more than 20 years. We have some innovative things coming up that I think will provide a significant growth pathway for us.

Thanks, Mike.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: BofA raises EQT price target on data center demand surge
data.description:
** BofA raises price target for natgas company EQT Corp to $80 from $63

** New PT represents 53.7% upside to the stock's last close

** With its scale and expansive midstream infrastructure, EQT is uniquely equipped to fulfill the intensive supply demands of large data centers while delivering seamless logistics support, brokerage says

** "We believe this structural advantage has allowed it to win coveted gas supply agreements" - BofA

** Brokerage expects EQT to win more data center deals, supporting the argument that inbasin data center demand is an idiosyncratic growthopportunity rather than a rising tide that lifts all boats

** 20 out of 25 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher and 5 "hold"; median PT is $65 - data compiled by LSEG

** Including session's moves, EQT shares up 12.8% YTD

data.title: CSX Teams with BNSF of Offer New Coast-to-Coast Service
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Railroad operators CSX and BNSF Railway have partnered to offer new intermodal service products in the U.S. that will connect the east and west coasts.

The arrangement creates new intermodal services linking Southern California to Charlotte, N.C., and Jacksonville, Fla., the companies said.

A new service will also be launched between Phoenix, Ariz., and Atlanta, Ga., that aims to convert over-the-road freight to rail.

More details about the services will be announced soon, the companies said.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com

data.title: Preliminary outcome indicates significantly oversubscribed rights issue
data.description:
Episurf

THIS PRESS RELEASE MAY NOT BE MADE PUBLIC, PUBLISHED OR DISTRIBUTED, DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY, IN OR INTO THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, THE UNITED KINGDOM, AUSTRALIA, BELARUS, HONG KONG, JAPAN, CANADA, ISRAEL, NEW ZEALAND, RUSSIA, SWITZERLAND, SINGAPORE, SOUTH AFRICA, SOUTH KOREA, OR ANY OTHER JURISDICTION WHERE SUCH DISTRIBUTION WOULD REQUIRE ADDITIONAL PROSPECTUS, REGISTRATION OR OTHER MEASURES IN ADDITION TO THOSE REQUIRED BY SWEDISH LAW, IS PROHIBITED, OR OTHERWISE IS UNLAWFUL OR CANNOT BE MADE WITHOUT THE APPLICATION OF AN EXEMPTION FROM SUCH ACTION. REFER TO THE SECTION "IMPORTANT INFORMATION" AT THE END OF THIS PRESS RELEASE.

Episurf Medical AB (publ) ("Episurf Medical", "Episurf" or the "Company"), (Nasdaq Stockholm: EPIS B) announced on July 2, 2025 that the Board of Directors had resolved, subject to the subsequent approval of the general meeting, to carry out an issue of units with preferential right for existing shareholders of up to approximately SEK 29.4 million (the "Rights Issue"). The Board of Directors' resolution on the Rights Issue was approved at the extraordinary general meeting of the Company held on August 4, 2025. Episurf hereby announces that the preliminary result of the Rights Issue indicates that 264,636,247 units, corresponding to approximately 162.1 percent of the Rights Issue, were subscribed for with and without unit rights. Accordingly, the guarantee commitments are not expected to be utilized. Episurf will receive proceeds amounting to approximately SEK 29.4 million before deduction of costs attributable to the Rights Issue.

The preliminary outcome of the Rights Issue indicates that 264,636,247 units, corresponding to approximately 162.1 percent of the Rights Issue, have been subscribed for with and without unit rights. Episurf will receive proceeds of approximately SEK 29.4 million before deduction of cost attributable to the Rights Issue.

The final outcome of the Rights Issue is expected to be announced in a press release on August 25, 2025.

Advisors

Episurf has engaged DNB Carnegie SME as financial advisor and Snellman Attorneys Ltd as legal advisor in connection with the Rights Issue.

For more information, please contact:

Pål Ryfors, CEO and interim CFO, Episurf Medical

Tel: +46 709 62 36 69

Email: pal.ryfors@episurf.com

About Episurf Medical

Episurf Medical is endeavoring to bring people with painful joint injuries a more active, healthier life through the availability of minimally invasive and individualised treatment alternatives. Episurf Medical's Episealer® individualised implants and Epiguide® surgical drill guides are developed for treating localised cartilage injury in joints. Episurf Medical's μiFidelity® system enables implants to be cost-efficiently tailored to each individual's unique injury for the optimal fit and minimal intervention. Episurf Medical's head office is in Stockholm, Sweden. Its share (EPIS B) is listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. For more information, go to the company's website: www.episurf.com.

This information is information that Episurf Medical AB is required to disclose pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014. The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the above contact person, at 17:40 CEST on August 22, 2025.

Important information

The release, announcement or distribution of this press release may, in certain jurisdictions, be subject to restrictions. The recipients of this press release in jurisdictions where this press release has been published or distributed shall inform themselves of and follow such legal restrictions. The recipient of this press release is responsible for using this press release, and the information contained herein, in accordance with applicable rules in each jurisdiction. This press release does not constitute an offer, or a solicitation of any offer, to buy or subscribe for any securities in Episurf Medical in any jurisdiction, neither from Episurf Medical nor from someone else.

This press release is not a prospectus for the purposes of Regulation (EU) 2017/1129 (the "Prospectus Regulation") and has not been approved by any regulatory authority in any jurisdiction. A prospectus, prepared in accordance with the simplified disclosure regime for secondary issuances as set forth in the Prospectus Regulation, regarding the Rights Issue described in this press release has been prepared by the Company, reviewed and approved by the Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority and published on the Company's website.

This announcement does not identify or suggest, or purport to identify or suggest, the risks (direct or indirect) that may be associated with an investment in the Company. The information contained in this announcement relating to the Rights Issue is for background purposes only and does not purport to be full or complete. No reliance may be placed for any purpose on the information contained in this announcement or its accuracy or completeness. DNB Carnegie SME are acting for Episurf Medical in connection with the Rights Issue and no one else and will not be responsible to anyone other than Episurf Medical for providing the protections afforded to its clients nor for giving advice in relation to the Rights Issue or any other matter referred to herein. DNB Carnegie SME are not liable to anyone else for providing the protection provided to their customers or for providing advice in connection with the Rights Issue or anything else mentioned herein.

This press release does not constitute or form part of an offer or solicitation to purchase or subscribe for securities in the United States. The securities referred to herein may not be sold in the United States absent registration or an exemption from registration under the US Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the "Securities Act"), and may not be offered or sold within the United States absent registration or an applicable exemption from, or in a transaction not subject to, the registration requirements of the Securities Act. There is no intention to register any securities referred to herein in the United States or to make a public Rights Issue of the securities in the United States. The information in this press release may not be announced, published, copied, reproduced or distributed, directly or indirectly, in whole or in part, within or into the US, the United Kingdom, Australia, Belarus, Canada, Hong Kong, Israel, Japan, New Zeeland, Russia, Singapore, South Africa, South Korea, Switzerland or in any other jurisdiction where such announcement, publication or distribution of the information would not comply with applicable laws and regulations or where such actions are subject to legal restrictions or would require additional registration or other measures than what is required under Swedish law. Actions taken in violation of this instruction may constitute a crime against applicable securities laws and regulations.

In the United Kingdom, this document and any other materials in relation to the securities described herein is only being distributed to, and is only directed at, and any investment or investment activity to which this document relates is available only to, and will be engaged in only with, "qualified investors" (within the meaning of the Prospectus Regulation as it forms part of domestic law in the United Kingdom by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018) who are (i) persons having professional experience in matters relating to investments who fall within the definition of "investment professionals" in Article 19(5) of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Financial Promotion) Order 2005 (the "Order"); or (ii) high net worth entities falling within Article 49(2)(a) to (d) of the Order (all such persons together being referred to as "relevant persons"). In the United Kingdom, any investment or investment activity to which this communication relates is available only to, and will be engaged in only with, relevant persons. Persons who are not relevant persons should not take any action on the basis of this press release and should not act or rely on it.

Forward-looking statements

This press release contains forward-looking statements that reflect the Company's intentions, beliefs, or current expectations about and targets for the Company's and the group's future results of operations, financial condition, liquidity, performance, prospects, anticipated growth, strategies and opportunities and the markets in which the Company and the group operates. data.title: Canada to Drop 25% Tariff on Some U.S. Imports — Source
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada on Friday will remove its 25% tariff on about half of the U.S. goods it has targeted since March, according to a person familiar with the matter, as a sign of goodwill from Ottawa as it tries to reset its trading relationship with the U.S.

However, Canadian tariffs on U.S. steel, aluminum and automobiles would remain in place, the person said.

The move comes the day after Prime Minister Mark Carney and President Trump spoke for the first time since the two countries failed to reach a trade deal prior to an Aug. 1 deadline. In a summary of the call released by Carney's office, the conversation was described as "productive," and the two leaders pledged to reconvene on trade.

Carney is scheduled to hold a press conference at noon ET. Earlier this month, he said that the government may remove some tariffs on U.S. goods as a way of helping domestic industries. The U.S. exempts Canadian imports from its 25% tariff so long as they comply with the terms in the U.S.-Mexico-Canada free trade treaty, known as USMCA.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Base Metal Prices Rise as Fed Chair Opens Door to Interest Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description: Base metal prices rise, with LME three-month copper up 0.6% at $9,790.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.8% at $2,613.0 a ton. Base metal prices have been boosted by weakness in the U.S. dollar, with demand increased as international purchasers find it cheaper to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Metals prices had drifted for much of the week, with few catalysts for traders to push prices in either direction. Recent dollar weakness follows Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's Jackson Hole summit address, in which Powell highlighted job market worries while tariffs drive up costs. Powell's speech effectively endorsed market expectations for a phase of cutting interest rates, ING analysts say in a note. This has pushed risk assets up, and the dollar down against a basket of other currencies, ING says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)
